title: Sorrowful Unto Death
subtitle: The first Compline of the Passion — Christ enters the garden of Gethsemane and begins to be afraid and heavy with grief

stillness: Be separated now in prayer, as Cyril says.
Withdraw apart, that you may learn:
with attentive mind and quiet heart one must speak with God on high.
gospel: Then Jesus came with them to a place called Gethsemane.
And when He had entered the garden, He said to His disciples:
Sit here while I go yonder and pray;
and you also pray, that you enter not into temptation.
And He took with Him Peter and the two sons of Zebedee.
And He began to be sorrowful and heavy.
Then He said to them: My soul is sorrowful even unto death.
And going a little farther He fell upon His face, praying and saying:
My Father, if this chalice cannot pass away unless I drink it, Thy will be done.
And being in agony, He prayed the longer.
And His sweat became as drops of blood, running down upon the ground.
behold: Take up these meditations from the very beginning of the Passion, and follow them in order.
And attend to each single thing as if you were present.
Behold, then, the Lord Jesus attentively,
as, going out from the supper at the hour of Compline,
He goes into the garden with His disciples,
now to be accompanied by them on this last journey;
see how tenderly, how companionably, how familiarly He speaks with them,
Behold Him whose all things are, in whom the adversary had nothing,
with death now drawing near, He begins to be sorrowful and heavy.
From afar, behold how into Himself He has carried over our weakness.
consider: In this — that He is afraid and grieves — we are taught to fear and grieve before the judgment of death.
This sorrow of the Lord was the greatest sorrow,
for His soul was as grieved as it could possibly be.
By nature He shuddered at death, and at the most bitter pains He foreknew He would soon suffer.
Yet know this: the Evangelists say He began to be sorrowful, He began to be afraid —
they do not say He was overcome, for the motion of sorrow did not master Him;
at once He took it up and subjected it to the divine will.
Nothing in Christ is reckoned as forced; all is believed to be willing.
Willing, He hungered and thirsted; willing, He feared and grieved.
And He was sorrowful not only at death which nature abhors,
but for the wretchedness of Judas, the scandal of the disciples, the ruin of Jerusalem —
having pity on these, and suffering with them.
And being in agony He prayed the longer, teaching us that in agony, in any distress whatever,
we must run back to prayer.
Why this, my God? Do You so suffer with me, showing Yourself man,
that in some way You seem not to know that You are God?
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, who for me, a wretch, willed to be sorrowful and heavy:
grant me to bear all my sorrows unceasingly up to You;
and deign to bear them together with me, in union with Your own sorrow.
O Jesus, who in prayer willed to be strengthened by an angel:
grant me, through the power of Your prayer,
that as I pray Your angel may ever stand by me, strengthening me.

latinJewel.latin: Unde hoc, Deus meus? Ita compateris mihi exhibens hominem, ut quodammodo videaris nescire quod Deus es?
latinJewel.english: Why this, my God? Do You so suffer with me, showing Yourself man, that in some way You seem not to know that You are God?
